notes: This is the source passage from which the Heart Sūtra's core section was extracted. The Heart Sūtra contains a paraphrase of this text, consistent with first having been translated into Chinese (by Kumārajīva as T223) and then incorporated into the Heart Sūtra composition.

parallel_passage.description: The passage where Subhūti describes the characteristics of dharmas in emptiness, which was extracted and condensed to form the core doctrinal section of the Heart Sūtra.

key_sections.sanskrit: rūpaṃ śūnyam, vedanā śūnyā, saṃjñā śūnyā, saṃskārāḥ śūnyāḥ, vijñānaṃ śūnyam
key_sections.note: Five aggregates are empty - source for Heart Sūtra negation series

key_sections.sanskrit: cakṣuḥ śūnyam, śrotraṃ śūnyam, ghrāṇaṃ śūnyam, jihvā śūnyā, kāyaḥ śūnyaḥ, manaḥ śūnyam
key_sections.note: Six sense faculties - source for āyatana negations
Eighteen elements - source for dhātu negations

key_sections.sanskrit: avidyā śūnyā... jarā-maraṇaṃ śūnyam. avidyā-nirodhaḥ śūnyaḥ... jarā-maraṇa-nirodhaḥ śūnyaḥ
key_sections.note: Dependent origination - NOTE: uses 'nirodha' (cessation), whereas Heart Sūtra Sanskrit uses 'kṣaya' (destruction), evidence of back-translation

key_sections.sanskrit: duḥkhaṃ śūnyam, samudayaḥ śūnyaḥ, nirodhaḥ śūnyaḥ, mārgaḥ śūnyaḥ

back_translation_evidence.analysis: The Large Prajñāpāramitā consistently uses 'nirodha' (cessation) for the ending of ignorance, old age and death, etc. The Heart Sūtra Sanskrit uses 'kṣaya' (destruction/exhaustion) instead. This is consistent with Nattier's thesis that the Sanskrit was back-translated from Chinese, where both terms can be rendered with the same character 盡 (jìn). The back-translator, unfamiliar with standard Prajñāpāramitā idiom, chose the wrong Sanskrit equivalent.

scholarly_refs: Nattier, Jan (1992). 'The Heart Sūtra: a Chinese apocryphal text?' JIABS 15(2): 153-223.
Karashima, Seishi et al. (2016). Gilgit Manuscripts in the National Archives of India. Tokyo.
